text:
Rigid 1,3-disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) serve as invaluable linear bioisosteres for para-substituted benzene rings, offering a strategy to enhance the pharmacokinetic profiles of drug candidates. However, the widespread adoption of BCPs in medicinal chemistry is hindered by the synthetic necessity of using [1.1.1]propellane—a highly unstable and cumbersome reagent that typically requires in-house preparation and immediate use. While more stable alternatives exist, they generally lack the versatile reactivity required for broad application.

In this work, we introduce a class of stable, thianthrenium-based BCP reagents that facilitate practical O-, N-, and C-alkylation reactions. These reagents significantly expand the scope of bicyclopentylation, surpassing the utility of existing methods and even [1.1.1]propellane itself. Their design leverages the unique redox and stereoelectronic properties of the thianthrene scaffold, which enables both the efficient synthesis of the reagents via strain release and their subsequent high reactivity. Upon single-electron reduction, the weak exocyclic C–S bond undergoes selective mesolytic cleavage to generate BCP radicals. These intermediates participate in transition metal-mediated bond formations, allowing for the installation of the BCP motif onto a wide array of functional groups, including the late-stage modification of complex, multistep synthetic intermediates.
